subject: Terry v. Ohio (1968) and the 'stop and frisk' / reasonable-suspicion standard
central_question: When can a police officer stop you on the street and search your body without a warrant and without proof of a crime — and where is the line between a hunch and the law?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how a single 1968 Supreme Court decision created the lower 'reasonable suspicion' standard that quietly governs millions of street stops in America today.
surprise: The police do not need a warrant or probable cause to stop and frisk you; a 1968 case built a whole second, lower standard for the street — and it started with one detective watching two men pace a sidewalk.
stakes: Whether an officer can stop and pat you down on the street, what counts as 'reasonable suspicion,' and how the balance between officer safety and individual liberty is enforced in everyday encounters.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in law, policing, rights and how systems work
demand_evidence: 'Know your rights' and police-encounter content is one of the largest live genres on YouTube; this case is the legal floor under all of it.
Landmark-case explainers sustain durable long-tail search interest.
competition: Most existing videos re-tell the case facts or argue modern stop-and-frisk politics; few center the neutral 'how the reasonable-suspicion standard was built' causal angle.
differentiation: Frames Terry as the origin of the everyday street stop (the hidden system), present-tense and self-relevant, with primary-source citations and a neutral, advertiser-safe tone. Opens the EP6-8 'surveillance / state-vs-you' trilogy.
research_feasibility: High: the opinion is public domain (392 U.S. 1); Cornell LII and Oyez provide the full opinion, syllabus and oral-argument records.
visual_feasibility: High: symbolic reconstruction of a street stop, a store window being cased, animated contrast of probable cause vs reasonable suspicion, timeline to 1968.
risk_notes: Decided landmark case (1968); parties deceased/institutional; opinion is public domain - advertiser-friendly.

Policing topic: keep strictly neutral (neither anti- nor pro-police). Treat later stop-and-frisk controversy as documented debate, not accusation; do not state a city/era practice as fact unless grounded.
Real people (Det. McFadden, John W. Terry, Richard Chilton) described by role, accurately as charged/convicted; no real-person likeness in AI visuals (invariant 11).
Generated visuals are illustration/reconstruction, never presented as authentic historical footage.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding and key facts to the primary opinion plus one independent reference.
